---
title: "Foundations: discrete geometric computation"
---

This chapter introduces the viewpoint that governs `nitrix.geometry` and the
mesh-oriented parts of `nitrix.sparse`: geometric computation in neuroimaging is
usually computation on a finite representation of a smooth object. A cortical
surface is stored as vertices and triangles, not as a differentiable manifold. A
brain image is stored as samples on a lattice, not as a function on
$\mathbb{R}^3$. Nevertheless, the quantities we want to compute - curvature,
area, gradients, Laplacians, deformations, and distances - are most naturally
defined in the continuous setting.

The central question is therefore not whether the discrete object is "the same"
as the continuous object. It is not. The question is whether the discrete
operation has been chosen so that, under refinement and under the assumptions of
the model, it approaches the continuous operation it claims to represent.

The reader is assumed to know linear algebra and basic multivariate calculus. No
formal background in differential geometry is required. We will use the language
of tangent planes, curvature, and Laplace--Beltrami operators, but each will be
introduced through finite-dimensional approximations: matrices, sparse
operators, local least-squares fits, and quadratic energies. A second theme runs
underneath the first: every good discrete operator is built to respect the
*transformations* under which the geometry is meant to be invariant. That theme,
developed in @sec-transformations-bias, is what connects this chapter to the
modern language of inductive bias and geometric learning.

## The Running Example: Curvature of Cortex {#sec-running-example}

Suppose we have reconstructed a cortical surface as a triangle mesh
$$
  \mathcal{M}_h = (V,F), \qquad
  V \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times 3}, \quad
  F \in \{0,\ldots,n-1\}^{m\times 3}.
$$
The rows of $V$ are vertex coordinates and the rows of $F$ are triples of
indices specifying triangular faces. The subscript $h$ is a reminder that this
mesh has a resolution: on a nearly uniform mesh, $h$ can be read as a typical
edge length.

We would like to compute a number at each vertex that says how the surface bends
there. On a smooth surface $S \subset \mathbb{R}^3$, the mean curvature $H$
and Gaussian curvature $K$ are differential quantities. Their definitions use
how a tangent plane changes from point to point. But a triangle mesh has flat
faces and sharp edges, so there is no classical tangent plane at an edge and no
second derivative at a vertex.

This is not a defect of the data structure. It is the problem. A useful mesh
curvature is a discrete quantity whose value on $\mathcal{M}_h$ converges to
the smooth curvature on $S$ as $h\to 0$, provided $\mathcal{M}_h$ is a
reasonable refinement of $S$. The same pattern recurs throughout the geometry
unit:

- define the continuous object;
- choose a finite representation;
- construct a discrete operator on that representation;
- state what convergence, invariance, or equivariance makes the construction
  meaningful;
- implement the operator with clear execution and differentiability contracts.

This is the geometry analogue of the modelling discipline used in the statistics
unit. A formula is not just a formula; it is also a claim about the data it is
allowed to act on, and about the transformations under which that claim is
supposed to hold.

## Discrete Objects and Continuous Claims {#sec-discretization}

Two representation families occur repeatedly in `nitrix`.

A **regular grid** represents samples of a scalar or vector field. For example, a
3-D image is an array $I[i,j,k]$ that samples some underlying intensity field
$I(x)$. The array alone does not know physical distance; routines such as
`spatial_gradient`, `marching_cubes`, `mesh_to_sdf`, and `ribbon_map` take an
explicit `spacing` argument when physical units matter.

A **triangle mesh** represents a surface. The finite-dimensional data are
vertices and faces. The implicit continuous claim is that these triangles are a
piecewise-flat approximation of a smooth surface. The mesh may come from an
icosphere, a cortical reconstruction, a marching-cubes extraction, or a template
deformation. The source affects what should be trusted: an icosphere is an
analytic test object, while a real cortical mesh carries reconstruction error,
topology constraints, and anatomical conventions.

The canonical test object is the **icosphere**: begin with a regular
icosahedron and repeatedly subdivide each triangle into four, projecting every
new edge midpoint back onto the unit sphere. Each round of subdivision is a
finer discrete stand-in for the *same* smooth surface, so the icosphere makes
"converges under refinement" something we can watch numerically. We use it as the
analytic oracle in @sec-validation; @fig-foundations-icosphere shows the first
few levels and the piecewise-flat membrane tightening onto the sphere.

```{python}
#| label: fig-foundations-icosphere
#| fig-cap: "A discrete object approximating a smooth one. Each panel is an icosphere at a different subdivision level - a piecewise-flat triangle mesh standing in for the smooth unit sphere. From left to right the mesh refines (12, 42, and 162 vertices); the flat membrane hugs the sphere ever more tightly. The craft of discrete geometry is to compute on the mesh so that geometric quantities converge to their values on the sphere as the mesh refines."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d.art3d import Poly3DCollection

# Build an icosphere by hand (the same midpoint-subdivide rule as
# nitrix.sparse.mesh.icosphere, reproduced so the figure is self-contained).
def icosahedron():
    phi = (1.0 + np.sqrt(5.0)) / 2.0
    v = np.array([
        [-1, phi, 0], [1, phi, 0], [-1, -phi, 0], [1, -phi, 0],
        [0, -1, phi], [0, 1, phi], [0, -1, -phi], [0, 1, -phi],
        [phi, 0, -1], [phi, 0, 1], [-phi, 0, -1], [-phi, 0, 1],
    ], dtype=np.float64)
    v /= np.linalg.norm(v, axis=1, keepdims=True)
    f = np.array([
        [0, 11, 5], [0, 5, 1], [0, 1, 7], [0, 7, 10], [0, 10, 11],
        [1, 5, 9], [5, 11, 4], [11, 10, 2], [10, 7, 6], [7, 1, 8],
        [3, 9, 4], [3, 4, 2], [3, 2, 6], [3, 6, 8], [3, 8, 9],
        [4, 9, 5], [2, 4, 11], [6, 2, 10], [8, 6, 7], [9, 8, 1],
    ], dtype=np.int64)
    return v, f

def subdivide(v, f):
    cache, verts = {}, v.tolist()
    def mid(i, j):
        key = (min(i, j), max(i, j))
        if key in cache:
            return cache[key]
        m = (v[i] + v[j]) / 2.0
        m /= np.linalg.norm(m)              # project back onto the sphere
        cache[key] = len(verts); verts.append(m.tolist())
        return cache[key]
    nf = []
    for a, b, c in f:
        ab, bc, ca = mid(a, b), mid(b, c), mid(c, a)
        nf += [[a, ab, ca], [b, bc, ab], [c, ca, bc], [ab, bc, ca]]
    return np.asarray(verts), np.asarray(nf, dtype=np.int64)

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(11, 3.8))
v, f = icosahedron()
for level in range(3):
    ax = fig.add_subplot(1, 3, level + 1, projection='3d')
    ax.add_collection3d(Poly3DCollection(
        v[f], facecolor='#9ecae1', edgecolor='#3182bd',
        linewidths=0.3, alpha=0.9))
    ax.set_xlim(-1, 1); ax.set_ylim(-1, 1); ax.set_zlim(-1, 1)
    ax.set_box_aspect((1, 1, 1)); ax.set_axis_off()
    ax.set_title(f"level {level}: {v.shape[0]} vertices", fontsize=10)
    v, f = subdivide(v, f)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

We will use the following abstract notation. Let $A$ be a continuous operator,
such as a derivative or a Laplace--Beltrami operator, and let $A_h$ be its
discrete approximation at resolution $h$. Let $P_h f$ denote the samples of a
smooth function $f$ on the discrete object. A basic consistency statement has
the form
$$
  A_h(P_h f) \longrightarrow P_h(Af)
  \qquad \text{as } h\to 0.
$$
This equation is not meant as a formal theorem for every routine in the package.
It is the standard by which the routines should be understood. The left-hand
side is what the program computes. The right-hand side is the continuous quantity
the program claims to approximate.

The rate of convergence matters. If the error is $O(h)$, halving the grid
spacing roughly halves the error. If the error is $O(h^2)$, halving the grid
spacing roughly quarters the error. In three dimensions, changing $h$ is
expensive: halving the voxel spacing multiplies the number of voxels by about
eight. A second-order approximation can therefore be the difference between a
useful algorithm and an impractical one.

## Transformations and Inductive Bias {#sec-transformations-bias}

A useful way to recognize the geometry of a problem is to ask which
transformations should preserve its meaning. If a surface is translated or
rotated in $\mathbb{R}^3$, its area and curvature should not change. Its
normal vectors should change, but in a controlled way: rotate the surface and
the normals rotate with it. If a mesh is stored with a different vertex order,
the numerical values should be permuted in the same way, not changed. If an
image is shifted by one voxel away from the boundary, a local finite-difference
operator should shift with it.

This is, in essence, Felix Klein's organizing idea: a geometry is determined by
the group of transformations under which its meaningful quantities are
unchanged. Euclidean geometry is what survives rigid motion; the geometry of a
graph or mesh is what survives relabelling its nodes; the geometry of the sphere
is what survives rotation. Choosing *which group acts* is choosing the geometry,
and - as we will see - choosing the inductive bias of any algorithm built on it.

Two precise definitions make this operational. Let a group $G$ act on inputs by
$x \mapsto g\cdot x$. A quantity $q$ is **invariant** to $G$ if it is unchanged
by the action,
$$
  q(g\cdot x) = q(x),
$$
and a map $F$ is **equivariant** if transforming the input transforms the output
the corresponding way,
$$
  F(g\cdot x) = \rho(g)\, F(x),
$$
where $\rho(g)$ is how the same group element acts on the *output* space.
Invariance is the special case $\rho(g)=\mathrm{id}$: the output does not move at
all. Equivariance is the more fundamental notion, and the one most discrete
operators are built to satisfy.

Three examples fix the ideas, and the third carries a subtlety worth stating
precisely.

- A **scalar field on a surface**, such as a curvature map, is *invariant* to
  rigid motion of the embedding: move the surface in space and the value at each
  material point is unchanged. The same field is *equivariant* to vertex
  relabelling: permute the vertices with a permutation $P$ and the curvature
  vector is permuted, $q(P\cdot \mathcal{M}) = P\,q(\mathcal{M})$.
- A **normal field** is *equivariant* to rotation: rotate the surface by $R$ and
  every normal rotates, $n \mapsto Rn$. It is *invariant* to translation.
- **Mean curvature** $H$ illustrates that the acting group must be named with
  care. $H$ is invariant under *orientation-preserving* rigid motions (the proper
  group $SE(3)$), but it carries a sign relative to a chosen normal, so under a
  *reflection* it flips, $H \mapsto -H$: it is equivariant, not invariant, under
  the full group $O(3)$ that includes reflections. Gaussian curvature $K$, a
  product of two signed principal curvatures, is invariant even under reflection.
  This is exactly why `geometry.mean_curvature` must fix a sign convention
  (positive = convex with respect to the outward normal) while
  `geometry.gaussian_curvature` need not.

These properties are not decorative. They determine what an algorithm is allowed
to learn or compute. A local stencil on a regular grid assumes that the same
operation should be reused at every spatial location - it is *translation
equivariant* by construction. A mesh operator built from adjacency, edge lengths,
angles, and areas assumes that storage order is irrelevant - it is *permutation
equivariant*. A spherical convolution whose weights depend on geodesic distance
assumes that nearby points on the sphere should interact in a way that does not
privilege the latitude-longitude grid - it respects the rotation group of the
sphere.

This is the bridge from geometry to inductive bias, and to what is now called
geometric (deep) learning. A model that uses `spherical_conv`, a cotangent
Laplacian, or `apply_operator` over an `ELL` adjacency does not have to *learn*
from data that vertex labels are arbitrary, that neighbourhoods matter, or that
smoothness should be measured along the surface rather than through the ambient
volume. Those commitments are built into the operator as equivariances. They
shrink the hypothesis space and rule out many functions that would be easy for a
generic array model to represent but meaningless for the geometry at hand. The
payoff is the usual one for a well-chosen prior: fewer parameters, less data, and
predictions that transform correctly on inputs never seen during fitting.

The same principle appears outside learned models. A registration regularizer is
an inductive bias over deformations: it says which transformations are plausible
before seeing the image evidence. A curvature estimator is an inductive bias over
local surface shape: it says which finite measurements are allowed to stand in
for a second derivative. The craft is not to avoid bias. The craft is to choose
the bias that matches the geometry of the object.

## A First Operator: Finite Differences {#sec-finite-differences}

The derivative of a one-dimensional function is the limit
$$
  f'(x) = \lim_{h\to 0} \frac{f(x+h)-f(x)}{h}.
$$
The most direct discrete approximation is the forward difference
$$
  (D_h^+ f)(x) = \frac{f(x+h)-f(x)}{h}.
$$
A Taylor expansion gives
$$
  f(x+h)
    = f(x) + h f'(x) + \frac{h^2}{2} f''(x) + O(h^3),
$$
and therefore
$$
  (D_h^+ f)(x)
    = f'(x) + \frac{h}{2}f''(x) + O(h^2).
$$
The leading error is proportional to $h$, so the method is first-order
accurate.

A small change improves the situation. The central difference is
$$
  (D_h^0 f)(x) = \frac{f(x+h)-f(x-h)}{2h}.
$$
Expanding both $f(x+h)$ and $f(x-h)$, the even-order terms cancel:
$$
  (D_h^0 f)(x)
    = f'(x) + \frac{h^2}{6}f'''(x) + O(h^4).
$$
The approximation is now second-order accurate. The improvement came from a
*symmetry*: centring the stencil makes the operator odd about the point, which
kills the even-order error term. This is the simplest instance of the theme of
@sec-transformations-bias - a transformation property of the operator (here,
symmetry under $h\mapsto -h$) is what buys the accuracy. Symmetry, conservation,
and equivariance are not aesthetic preferences; they remove leading error terms
and determine which functions the operator can represent.

In `nitrix`, `geometry.spatial_gradient` implements this idea for scalar fields
on regular grids. Its default scheme is the central difference. The `sobel` and
`scharr` schemes use the same derivative idea but add smoothing across the
off-axes, improving robustness to noise while preserving a differentiable array
operator. The output appends a final derivative-direction axis, so an input of
shape `(..., *spatial)` produces `(..., *spatial, ndim)`.

```{python}
#| label: fig-finite-difference-orders
#| fig-cap: "Forward and central finite differences approximate the same derivative but converge at different rates. The plot shows the error in approximating d/dx sin(x) at x=1. The central difference cancels the leading error term and follows a second-order trend."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x0 = 1.0
truth = np.cos(x0)
hs = np.logspace(-0.25, -3.0, 16)

forward = np.array([
    abs((np.sin(x0 + h) - np.sin(x0)) / h - truth)
    for h in hs
])
central = np.array([
    abs((np.sin(x0 + h) - np.sin(x0 - h)) / (2 * h) - truth)
    for h in hs
])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.5, 4.2))
ax.loglog(hs, forward, "o-", label="forward difference")
ax.loglog(hs, central, "s-", label="central difference")
ax.loglog(hs, forward[0] * (hs / hs[0]), "k:", lw=1, label="slope 1")
ax.loglog(hs, central[0] * (hs / hs[0]) ** 2, "k--", lw=1, label="slope 2")
ax.set_xlabel("grid spacing h")
ax.set_ylabel("absolute error")
ax.invert_xaxis()
ax.grid(True, which="both", alpha=0.3)
ax.legend()
plt.show()
```

The point of this figure is not that all geometry algorithms reduce to finite
differences. They do not. The point is that a discrete formula should be read as
a convergence claim. Once that habit is in place, mesh Laplacians and curvature
operators become less mysterious.

## Mesh Operators as Matrices, Energies, and Geometry {#sec-mesh-operators}

Let $f \in \mathbb{R}^n$ be a scalar value at each mesh vertex. A mesh operator
is often a sparse matrix acting on $f$. The same object can be understood from
several complementary views, and a builder gains the most by holding all of them
at once.

First, the **linear algebra view**: an operator $L\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}$
maps a per-vertex field to another per-vertex field. If $X\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times
3}$ is the vertex-coordinate matrix, the same operator can be applied to each
coordinate column.

Second, the **local averaging view**: the value $(Lf)_i$ compares $f_i$ with
the values around vertex $i$. A Laplacian should be zero on constant fields and
large where $f_i$ differs from its neighbourhood.

Third, the **energy view**: for the positive semidefinite stiffness form used in
`mesh_cotangent_laplacian`,
$$
  f^\top L f
    = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{(i,j)} w_{ij}(f_i-f_j)^2
$$
when the weights are symmetric. This says that $L$ measures roughness. Smooth
fields have small energy; oscillatory fields have large energy.

These are not different subjects. They are different lenses on the same sparse
matrix, and the point of naming them together is that a result proven in one view
holds in the others. A fourth lens makes the connection to
@sec-transformations-bias explicit - the **symmetry view**: a valid mesh operator
must not depend on the accidental ordering of vertices and faces. If a
permutation matrix $P$ relabels the vertices, the relabelled operator is
$PLP^\top$, and applying it to the relabelled field gives
$$
  (PLP^\top)(Pf)=P(Lf).
$$
This is the algebraic statement that $L$ is **permutation equivariant**: it is
the same operator no matter how its rows are stored. The mesh is geometry; the
row order in an array is storage.

The simplest mesh Laplacian uses equal weights over the one-ring neighbourhood
$\mathcal{N}(i)$. This is the umbrella or graph Laplacian. It is useful for
some smoothing operations, but it is not sufficiently geometric: it treats a long
thin triangle and a nearly equilateral triangle as if they contributed the same
local information.

The standard geometric improvement is the cotangent Laplacian. For an interior
edge $(i,j)$, let $\alpha_{ij}$ and $\beta_{ij}$ be the two angles opposite
that edge in the adjacent triangles. Define
$$
  w_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}
    \left(\cot \alpha_{ij} + \cot \beta_{ij}\right).
$$
The positive stiffness form is then
$$
  (Lf)_i = \sum_{j\in\mathcal{N}(i)}
    w_{ij}(f_i - f_j).
$$
This construction uses the geometry of the triangles, not only their
connectivity. It is the finite-element/discrete-exterior-calculus operator used
by `sparse.mesh_cotangent_laplacian`. The operator is assembled on the host,
because the neighbourhood structure and maximum row degree are data-dependent;
once assembled as `ELL` or `SectionedELL`, its matrix-vector application is a
JAX operation through `sparse.apply_operator`. The cotangent weights also encode
the intended transformation behaviour: rigidly moving the surface leaves them
unchanged; uniformly scaling the surface leaves the angles, and therefore the
stiffness weights, unchanged, while the mass matrix scales like area. The
mass-normalized quantities then acquire the expected physical units, such as
curvature scaling like inverse length.

The mass matrix is the companion of the stiffness matrix. In continuous
geometry, many formulas integrate over area:
$$
  \int_S f(x)\,dA.
$$
On a mesh, `vertex_areas(mesh)` assigns an area $A_i$ to each vertex, yielding
a diagonal lumped mass matrix $M=\operatorname{diag}(A_i)$. The barycentric
area rule gives each triangle's area equally to its three vertices. The default
mixed-Voronoi rule is more accurate on well-shaped triangles and has an
obtuse-triangle branch that is important for real cortical meshes.

Together, stiffness and mass form the discrete analogue of many continuous
operators. For example, the generalized eigenproblem
$$
  L\phi = \lambda M\phi
$$
is the mesh analogue of the Laplace--Beltrami eigenproblem. The same pair also
appears in implicit smoothing, curvature, and spectral surface analysis.

## Curvature from the Laplacian {#sec-curvature-from-laplacian}

We can now return to the running example. Let $X\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times 3}$ be
the vertex-coordinate matrix. Applying the cotangent stiffness to $X$ produces
a vector at each vertex:
$$
  (LX)_i
    = \sum_{j\in\mathcal{N}(i)}
      w_{ij}(x_i-x_j).
$$
After division by vertex area, this approximates the mean-curvature normal:
$$
  M^{-1}LX \approx 2H n,
$$
where $n$ is the outward unit normal and $H$ is the scalar mean curvature.
Consequently `geometry.mean_curvature` computes, in effect,
$$
  H_i
    = \frac{1}{2}\,
      \operatorname{sign}\!\left(
        (M^{-1}LX)_i \cdot n_i
      \right)
      \left\|(M^{-1}LX)_i\right\|.
$$
The sign convention is documented in the implementation: positive curvature is
convex with respect to the outward vertex normal, so a sphere is positive and
sulcal fundi are negative under the usual cortical orientation. (This is the sign
choice forced by the $O(3)$ subtlety of @sec-transformations-bias.) FreeSurfer's
`?h.curv` uses the opposite sign convention, so comparisons require a sign flip.

Gaussian curvature has a different discrete origin. At a vertex $i$, add the
interior angles of all incident triangles. The angle defect
$$
  \Omega_i = 2\pi - \sum_{\text{faces incident to } i}\theta_{if}
$$
is the integrated Gaussian curvature concentrated at that vertex. Dividing by
the vertex area gives
$$
  K_i = \frac{\Omega_i}{A_i}.
$$
This is implemented by `geometry.gaussian_curvature`. The integrated identity
$$
  \sum_i K_i A_i = 2\pi\chi
$$
is the discrete Gauss--Bonnet theorem. For a closed genus-zero mesh,
$\chi=2$, so the sum is $4\pi$. This is not merely a numerical target; for
the angle-defect construction it is a combinatorial identity, up to floating
point arithmetic. It therefore holds *exactly* at every refinement level - there
is no convergence march to watch, which is itself the lesson: a topological
invariant is reproduced exactly, while metric quantities like area and mean
curvature only converge.

The principal curvatures are then recovered from
$$
  H = \frac{\kappa_1+\kappa_2}{2}, \qquad
  K = \kappa_1\kappa_2,
$$
so
$$
  \kappa_{1,2}
    = H \pm \sqrt{\max(H^2-K,0)}.
$$
This closed form is why `geometry.principal_curvatures` does not need an
eigensolver at each vertex - a point we return to in @sec-devices, since avoiding
the eigensolver is also what keeps the routine off the fragile dense-GPU solver
path.

## Oracles and Validation {#sec-validation}

A geometry library should not be validated by bit-for-bit agreement with another
library. Two implementations can make different legitimate discretization
choices and still converge to the same continuous object. The correct validation
question is: which oracle is appropriate?

An **analytic oracle** is available when the smooth answer is known. The unit
sphere is the most useful example. For a sphere of radius $r$,
$$
  \operatorname{area}=4\pi r^2,\qquad
  H=\frac{1}{r},\qquad
  K=\frac{1}{r^2},\qquad
  \int_S K\,dA=4\pi.
$$
The `sparse.icosphere(n_iterations)` constructor supplies a controlled sequence
of mesh refinements, with vertex count $10\cdot 4^n+2$. It is therefore a
natural object for convergence tests: area should approach $4\pi r^2$, mean
curvature should approach $1/r$, and the angle-defect integral should satisfy
Gauss--Bonnet.

A **real-data oracle** is different. On an empirical cortical surface there is no
closed-form curvature. Comparisons against tools such as FreeSurfer should use
class, sign, and correlation checks, not equality. A high but imperfect
correlation can be the expected result when the reference uses smoothing, a
different curvature estimator, a different resampling path, or a different sign
convention. The residual should be explained, not hidden; but demanding bit
parity would test an arbitrary implementation choice rather than the underlying
geometry.

@fig-foundations-validation shows both postures side by side: the analytic-oracle
convergence on the icosphere (left), where the right thing to watch is the error
marching to zero under refinement, and the real-data gate (right), where the
right thing to watch is correlation and matched sign across many cases, not the
unattainable $y=x$ bit-parity line.

```{python}
#| label: fig-foundations-validation
#| fig-cap: "The two-oracle posture. Left: against the analytic oracle (the unit sphere), two metric quantities computed on the icosphere - total surface area and the cotangent mean-curvature estimate - converge to $4\\pi r^2$ and $H=1/r$ under refinement, both riding a second-order (slope-2) trend; halving the edge length quarters the error. (The bare icosahedron is vertex-transitive, so its mean-curvature mean is already exact by symmetry; the genuine march begins one level in.) Right: against a reference tool there is no closed form, so the acceptance gate is high correlation and matched sign across many test cases, not the unattainable $y=x$ bit-parity line."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d.art3d import Poly3DCollection

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

# --- Analytic oracle: convergence on the icosphere --------------------------
def icosahedron():
    phi = (1.0 + np.sqrt(5.0)) / 2.0
    v = np.array([
        [-1, phi, 0], [1, phi, 0], [-1, -phi, 0], [1, -phi, 0],
        [0, -1, phi], [0, 1, phi], [0, -1, -phi], [0, 1, -phi],
        [phi, 0, -1], [phi, 0, 1], [-phi, 0, -1], [-phi, 0, 1],
    ], dtype=np.float64)
    v /= np.linalg.norm(v, axis=1, keepdims=True)
    f = np.array([
        [0, 11, 5], [0, 5, 1], [0, 1, 7], [0, 7, 10], [0, 10, 11],
        [1, 5, 9], [5, 11, 4], [11, 10, 2], [10, 7, 6], [7, 1, 8],
        [3, 9, 4], [3, 4, 2], [3, 2, 6], [3, 6, 8], [3, 8, 9],
        [4, 9, 5], [2, 4, 11], [6, 2, 10], [8, 6, 7], [9, 8, 1],
    ], dtype=np.int64)
    return v, f

def subdivide(v, f):
    cache, verts = {}, v.tolist()
    def mid(i, j):
        key = (min(i, j), max(i, j))
        if key in cache:
            return cache[key]
        m = (v[i] + v[j]) / 2.0
        m /= np.linalg.norm(m)
        cache[key] = len(verts); verts.append(m.tolist())
        return cache[key]
    nf = []
    for a, b, c in f:
        ab, bc, ca = mid(a, b), mid(b, c), mid(c, a)
        nf += [[a, ab, ca], [b, bc, ab], [c, ca, bc], [ab, bc, ca]]
    return np.asarray(verts), np.asarray(nf, dtype=np.int64)

def total_area(v, f):
    a, b, c = v[f[:, 0]], v[f[:, 1]], v[f[:, 2]]
    return 0.5 * np.linalg.norm(np.cross(b - a, c - a), axis=1).sum()

def mean_curvature_error(v, f):
    # cotangent mean-curvature normal: H_i = |Lv|_i / (4 A_i), the discrete
    # estimator nitrix.geometry.mean_curvature uses. Analytic truth on the
    # unit sphere is H = 1/r = 1, so we track |mean(H) - 1|.
    n = v.shape[0]
    Lv = np.zeros_like(v)
    area = np.zeros(n)
    for i, j, k in f:
        for a, b, c in ((i, j, k), (j, k, i), (k, i, j)):
            u, w = v[a] - v[c], v[b] - v[c]          # angle at vertex c
            cot = np.dot(u, w) / np.linalg.norm(np.cross(u, w))
            Lv[a] += cot * (v[a] - v[b])
            Lv[b] += cot * (v[b] - v[a])
        area[[i, j, k]] += np.linalg.norm(
            np.cross(v[j] - v[i], v[k] - v[i])) / 6.0  # barycentric third
    H = np.linalg.norm(Lv, axis=1) / (4.0 * area)
    return abs(H.mean() - 1.0)

v, f = icosahedron()
h, area_err, curv_err = [], [], []
for _ in range(6):
    h.append(np.linalg.norm(v[f[:, 0]] - v[f[:, 1]], axis=1).mean())
    area_err.append(abs(total_area(v, f) - 4 * np.pi) / (4 * np.pi))
    curv_err.append(mean_curvature_error(v, f))
    v, f = subdivide(v, f)
h, area_err, curv_err = map(np.array, (h, area_err, curv_err))

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 4.4))
axL.loglog(h, area_err, 'o-', color='#3182bd', label=r'surface area $\to 4\pi r^2$')
# the level-0 icosahedron is vertex-transitive: its mean curvature is exact by
# symmetry (machine zero), so plot the genuine march from level 1 on.
axL.loglog(h[1:], curv_err[1:], 's-', color='#e6550d', label=r'mean curvature $\to H=1/r$')
axL.loglog(h, area_err[0] * (h / h[0]) ** 2, 'k--', lw=1, alpha=0.6, label='slope 2')
axL.set_xlabel('mean edge length $h$  (coarse -> fine)')
axL.set_ylabel('error vs the analytic truth')
axL.set_title('analytic oracle: convergence under refinement', fontsize=10)
axL.legend(fontsize=8, loc='lower right'); axL.grid(True, which='both', alpha=0.3)

# --- Real-data oracle: correlation + sign, not bit-parity -------------------
n = 200
latent = rng.normal(size=n)                       # the continuum quantity
ours = latent + 0.06 * rng.normal(size=n)         # our estimator
ref = latent + 0.06 * rng.normal(size=n)          # a reference tool
r = np.corrcoef(ours, ref)[0, 1]
axR.scatter(ours, ref, s=14, alpha=0.6, color='#31a354', edgecolor='none')
lim = [min(ours.min(), ref.min()), max(ours.max(), ref.max())]
axR.plot(lim, lim, 'k--', lw=1, alpha=0.5, label='$y=x$ (bit-parity, unattainable)')
axR.set_xlabel('nitrix estimate (per test case)')
axR.set_ylabel('reference tool estimate')
axR.set_title(f'real-data oracle: class / correlation (r = {r:.2f})', fontsize=10)
axR.legend(fontsize=8, loc='upper left'); axR.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
axR.set_aspect('equal', 'box')
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

This distinction is a useful guardrail for contributors. If a test uses an
analytic oracle, it should usually become sharper under refinement. If a test
uses real data, it should state the accepted qualitative property or correlation
floor and explain the remaining discrepancy. If a test demands bit-parity with
another tool, it is almost always testing the wrong thing.

## Execution Classes {#sec-classes}

The mathematical distinction between construction and application becomes an
engineering distinction in JAX. Some work can live inside `jit`, `vmap`, and
`grad`; some work must happen on the host before or after the traced computation.
The single operational fact a routine's class tells you is whether you may call
it inside a jitted, differentiated optimization loop. The geometry documentation
uses three execution labels.

| Class | Meaning | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| `DIFF-JAX` | Fixed-shape, differentiable array computation. Safe in the traced hot path. | `spatial_gradient`, `vertex_areas`, `mean_curvature`, `gaussian_curvature`, `surface_smooth`, `deform_to_sdf`, `apply_operator` |
| `HOST-CTOR` | Host-side construction whose output shape or topology depends on the data. It may produce arrays later consumed by JAX. | `icosphere`, `icosphere_hierarchy`, `mesh_k_ring_adjacency`, `mesh_cotangent_laplacian`, `marching_cubes`, `mesh_to_sdf` |
| `HOST-QA` | Host-side query or quality-assurance step, usually not part of a differentiable objective. | `euler_characteristic`, `genus`, `find_self_intersections`, `remove_self_intersections` |

The boundary that decides `DIFF-JAX` from `HOST` is **shape**. JAX traces a
function to a fixed computation graph, so every array shape must be known at
trace time. A curvature value has the same shape as the vertex set - known - so
curvature is `DIFF-JAX`. A level set has as many vertices as the volume happens
to produce - unknown until you look - so marching cubes must be `HOST-CTOR`. The
rule is simple and load-bearing:

> A `HOST-CTOR` or `HOST-QA` operation must not be called inside a jitted loop.

For example, constructing a cotangent operator from mesh topology is host-side
work. Applying the already-constructed operator to a per-vertex field is
differentiable JAX work. In an optimizer, build the sparse operator before the
loop, then apply it inside the loop. If an algorithm must rebuild topology or
perform host cleanup, it should do so between traced chunks, not inside a
`lax.fori_loop`.

There is one subtle case worth keeping in mind. A host-built cotangent operator
contains weights that are constants with respect to the traced computation. This
is correct when applying a fixed operator to many fields. It is not correct when
the loss must differentiate through the dependence of the cotangent weights on
the vertex positions. For that case, `geometry.mean_curvature` uses a JAX-native
per-face cotangent apply internally, so the weights remain differentiable with
respect to `mesh.vertices` (the two paths agreeing to about $10^{-4}$).

## Sparse Storage and Pytrees {#sec-storage-pytrees}

`nitrix` separates sparse storage from sparse algebra. The principal storage
format is `ELL`: each row stores up to a fixed number of column indices and
values. This is efficient for near-uniform degree structures such as icospheres.
For irregular valence, `SectionedELL` groups rows by degree so that high-degree
rows do not force every row to carry the same padding. The common entry point
`sparse.apply_operator(op, x)` accepts either representation and applies it to a
per-vertex field of shape `(..., n, d)`. The full development of both formats and
the semiring algebra they carry is the subject of the
[next chapter](operators.qmd#sec-ell).

`Mesh`, `ELL`, and `SectionedELL` are registered JAX pytrees. This means they can
cross `jit`, `vmap`, and `grad` boundaries as structured operands rather than as
opaque Python objects. For `Mesh(vertices, faces)`, both leaves are arrays.
Gradients flow through `vertices`; the integer `faces` array is topology and has
a zero/`float0` cotangent. This design avoids treating large face arrays as
static metadata, which would be unhashable and would cause unnecessary
recompilation for every topology. Differentiating through the whole mesh (rather
than only the vertices) requires `jax.grad(f, allow_int=True)` so JAX admits the
integer `faces` leaf.

`IcosphereHierarchy` is deliberately not a pytree. It is host-side construction
metadata: a sequence of meshes plus parent tables used to build cross-level
pooling and upsampling operators. It should be consumed to produce concrete
operators, not carried through traced numerical code.

## Device and Numerical Discipline {#sec-devices}

The geometry tier makes three device-level bets that shape its whole design, and
they are worth understanding before reading any routine because they explain why
the code looks the way it does.

First, **cuSolver-free by construction**. On real hardware the dense GPU LAPACK
stack (cuSolver) is not always trustworthy: certain CUDA/JAX combinations ship a
broken solver handle in which `eigh`, `qr`, `cholesky`, `solve`, `inv`, and `svd`
all fail at runtime while the rest of the device works. The suite therefore
avoids it. Small formulas are written in closed form - principal curvatures use
the quadratic formula $\kappa = H \pm \sqrt{H^2-K}$ of @sec-curvature-from-laplacian,
never an eigensolver. Large smoothing systems use matrix-free conjugate gradient
(`linalg.krylov.cg`), which needs only matrix-vector products, never a
factorization; the backward-Euler smoothing solve $(M + tL)x = Mx_0$ rides it.
Where a dense factorization is genuinely unavoidable elsewhere in the library, it
passes through the `safe_*` solver seam, which probes a tiny GPU call once and
falls back to CPU - announced, never silent - the first time the GPU solver
fails, moving the result back to the originating device.

Second, **XLA, not custom kernels, for the mesh/semiring tier**. The mesh layer
lowers onto a gather-and-reduce (`semiring_ell_matmul`): for each output row,
read the feature rows of that row's neighbours and reduce them under a semiring.
XLA lowers this `gather`/`scatter` + `fori_loop` pattern efficiently. The pinned
Pallas GPU backend does not lower the `gather` primitive at all, so the substrate
bet is XLA and the tier ships no custom kernels. The full rationale, and the
conditions under which that might change, live in
[the backend page](performance.qmd#sec-backend).

Third, **everything stays differentiable**. The data containers are registered
pytrees, so a `Mesh` flows through `jit`/`vmap`/`grad` as a first-class operand
and `jax.grad` of a loss over a `Mesh` returns a `Mesh`-structured cotangent. The
few routines built around an inner solve or fixed point expose a correct gradient
through implicit differentiation - a `custom_vjp` that differentiates the
*solution* of the system rather than unrolling the iteration. Together these mean
a discrete-geometry computation can sit inside a loss and the underlying surface
can be optimized by gradient descent. These three bets interlock with the
execution classes: the `DIFF-JAX` kernels are exactly the ones written to be
cuSolver-free and XLA-lowerable, and pytree registration is what lets a `Mesh` be
a `DIFF-JAX` operand at all.

## Conventions {#sec-conventions}

Several representation conventions are fixed throughout the unit.

**Index space.** Grid coordinates are index coordinates unless an explicit
`spacing` or affine transform is supplied. The physical position of index $i$
is determined by the calling convention, not by a hidden affine.

**Channels last.** Per-vertex fields use shape `(..., n, d)`, where `d` is the
feature dimension and leading axes are batch axes. A scalar field should use a
trailing singleton feature axis when passed to `apply_operator`. For
`spatial_gradient`, the trailing axes are spatial, so multi-channel images should
move channels into a leading batch axis before the call.

**Matrix indexing for grids.** Multi-axis grids use `indexing='ij'`, matching
array axis order. The alternative `xy` convention silently swaps the first two
axes and is a common source of transposed volumes.

**High-precision assembly where it matters.** Numerically sensitive host
construction, especially cotangent weights on nearly degenerate triangles, is
assembled in float64 and then cast back to the working dtype for device
application. This spends precision where conditioning is worst while keeping the
bulk computation in the requested dtype.

**Topology is not geometry.** Faces specify which vertices are connected.
Vertices specify where they are in space. Many algorithms require both, but they
are not interchangeable. Changing vertex positions with fixed faces deforms a
surface. Changing faces changes the discrete topology and usually changes the
operator shape.

## Reading Forward {#sec-reading-forward}

The next chapters refine the abstractions introduced here.

- [The operator layer](operators.qmd) develops ELL, SectionedELL, semirings, and
  mesh adjacency construction.
- [The Laplace--Beltrami operator](laplace-beltrami.qmd) studies the cotangent
  stiffness, the mass matrix, and smoothing in detail.
- [Curvature and distortion](curvature.qmd) derives the mean, Gaussian, and
  principal curvature routines and the surface-distortion measures used in
  cortical workflows.
- [Volume-surface conversion](volume-surface.qmd) explains marching cubes,
  signed-distance fields, topology checks, and the boundary between host
  construction and differentiable surface motion.

## Further Reading

- Crane, K. (2018). *Discrete Differential Geometry: An Applied Introduction.*
  A geometric entry point to cotangent Laplacians, angle defect, and curvature.
- Meyer, M., Desbrun, M., Schröder, P., & Barr, A. H. (2003). *Discrete
  Differential-Geometry Operators for Triangulated 2-Manifolds.* The source for
  the mixed-Voronoi area and cotangent mean-curvature normal used throughout the
  surface routines.
- Botsch, M., Kobbelt, L., Pauly, M., Alliez, P., & Lévy, B. (2010). *Polygon
  Mesh Processing.* A practical reference for mesh representations and
  algorithms.
- do Carmo, M. P. (1976). *Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces.* The
  smooth theory the discrete operators approximate - mean and Gaussian curvature,
  and the Gauss--Bonnet theorem.
- LeVeque, R. J. (2007). *Finite Difference Methods for Ordinary and Partial
  Differential Equations.* Background on consistency, order of accuracy, and
  convergence for grid operators.
- Bronstein, M. M., Bruna, J., Cohen, T., & Veličković, P. (2021). *Geometric
  Deep Learning: Grids, Groups, Graphs, Geodesics, and Gauges.* The modern,
  Erlangen-programme view of invariance and equivariance as the organizing
  principle of geometric models - the framing of @sec-transformations-bias.

## See also

- Source - the `Mesh` pytree, the icosphere, and the mesh operator/measure layer:
  [`src/nitrix/sparse/mesh.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/mesh.py).
- Source - the geometry package surface (the full public API this page surveys):
  [`src/nitrix/geometry/__init__.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/geometry/__init__.py).
- Source - the cuSolver-robust `safe_*` device seam:
  [`src/nitrix/linalg/_solver.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/linalg/_solver.py).
- Source - the format-agnostic apply seam:
  [`src/nitrix/sparse/_apply.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/_apply.py).
- Design contracts (execution classes, device posture):
  [`docs/design/geometry-suite.md`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/docs/design/geometry-suite.md).
- Next in reading order: [the operator layer](operators.qmd#sec-ell), then
  [the cotangent Laplacian](laplace-beltrami.qmd) and
  [curvature](curvature.qmd). Sibling unit:
  [the `nitrix.stats` foundations page](../statistics/foundations.qmd).
